summary:
The maxillary first molar typically features two major fossae—the central and lingual fossae—and one minor fossa located between the remaining cusps. This anatomical configuration is consistent across standard dental references, reflecting a total of three fossae where only two are considered primary or significant for clinical assessment.

The correct answer based on this analysis is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

Okay, let me now go through each choice:

A – Two fossae
B – Three fossae  
C – One major and two minor fossae  
D – Four fossae  

Option A suggests just two fossae but ignores the presence of at least one additional depression. Option B implies all three fissures count equally without distinguishing their significance clinically. Option D overstates the complexity beyond what’s observed structurally. Among options, "one major and two minor" best reflects both structural reality and typical classification used by clinicians when assessing caries risk or restorative planning due to varying depths and accessibility. Hence, selecting C provides an accurate balance between gross morphology and practical application within dentistry education standards.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{C}}$

We have already concluded earlier that there are essentially three fossae present—but not all carry equal importance during diagnosis or treatment decisions such as preventive care strategies involving sealants or cavity preparation techniques because some areas may be more vulnerable than others depending upon depth, location relative to occlusal surfaces, etc., especially considering how enamel thickness varies around different parts of tooth surface including near cervical margins which can influence decay progression patterns significantly even if they appear superficially similar under visual inspection alone.

Therefore, while technically having multiple depressions forming spaces between adjacent cusps—including those less prominent—it remains essential in practice to recognize specific named regions like central and lingual fossas rather than treating every small groove uniformly—as doing so could lead misjudgment about patient-specific needs related specifically toward targeted interventions aimed improving oral health outcomes via early detection methods before irreversible damage occurs from demineralisation processes initiated primarily along high-risk zones identified historically through extensive observation studies conducted throughout decades spanning various populations globally involved in epidemiological research projects focused mainly monitoring prevalence rates associated with common pathologies affecting permanent teeth particularly incisors up until third molars development stages inclusive thereof...

So again confirming once more why choosing “One Major & Two Minor” makes sense here—not merely counting physical gaps but understanding functional relevance tied closely into daily diagnostic
